coredata.dc:title: Novel ternary Z scheme carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated WS2/PANI ((CQDs@WS2/PANI):0D:2D:1D) nanocomposite for the photocatalytic degradation and electrochemical detection of pharmaceutical drugs

Husain, Samina
Khanuja, Manika
coredata.dc:description: The endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) and antibiotics are causing negative effects on human beings and animals by disrupting the endocrine system and spreading antimicrobial resistance. The current need is to eradicate pharmaceutical waste from water bodies using advanced catalytic systems with high efficiency. Novel ternary carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated Z-Scheme WS2-PANI nanocomposite was prepared by a green synthesis assisted in-situ polymerization for the photodegradation and detection of Estradiol (EST) and Nitrofurantoin (NFT). HRTEM micrographs revealed the formation of CQDs with a mean size of 4 ​nm anchored on the surface of WS2/PANI (width:PANI ​∼ ​20–30 ​nm). The ternary nanocomposite showed excellent photocatalytic activity, degraded NFT (95.7 ​% in 60 ​min), and EST (96.6 ​% in 60 ​min). The rate kinetics study confirms the reaction followed pseudo first-order model. This heterostructure exhibited enhanced performances by modulating the energy level configuration, enhancing the absorption of visible light (2.4 ​eV), and significantly improving the charge separation, three times higher than pristine WS2. These are highly favorable for increasing the generation of photoinduced charges and enhancing the overall performance of the catalyst. Further, the electrochemical sensor was prepared using CQDs@WS2/PANI nanocomposite on a paper-based electrode. The CQDs@WS2/PANI exhibit a linear response of 0.1–100 ​nM, with a limit of detection of 13 ​nM. This synergistic interfacial interaction resulted in the significantly improved electrochemical performance of the modified electrode. The proposed Z-scheme was justified by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and scavenger experiment. An intermediate degradation pathway was also proposed. The synthesized materials were characterized using FESEM, HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, UV-visible spectroscopy, PL, and TRPL. Therefore, this study provides a direct approach to fabricate a heterojunction that combines two-dimensional, one dimensional, and zero-dimensional properties, enabling control over the energy level configuration and subsequent improvements in photocatalytic and electrocatalytic efficiency.

head.ce:title: Novel ternary Z scheme carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated WS2/PANI ((CQDs@WS2/PANI):0D:2D:1D) nanocomposite for the photocatalytic degradation and electrochemical detection of pharmaceutical drugs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) and antibiotics are causing negative effects on human beings and animals by disrupting the endocrine system and spreading antimicrobial resistance. The current need is to eradicate pharmaceutical waste from water bodies using advanced catalytic systems with high efficiency. Novel ternary carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated Z-Scheme WS2-PANI nanocomposite was prepared by a green synthesis assisted in-situ polymerization for the photodegradation and detection of Estradiol (EST) and Nitrofurantoin (NFT). HRTEM micrographs revealed the formation of CQDs with a mean size of 4 ​nm anchored on the surface of WS2/PANI (width:PANI ​∼ ​20–30 ​nm). The ternary nanocomposite showed excellent photocatalytic activity, degraded NFT (95.7 ​% in 60 ​min), and EST (96.6 ​% in 60 ​min). The rate kinetics study confirms the reaction followed pseudo first-order model. This heterostructure exhibited enhanced performances by modulating the energy level configuration, enhancing the absorption of visible light (2.4 ​eV), and significantly improving the charge separation, three times higher than pristine WS2. These are highly favorable for increasing the generation of photoinduced charges and enhancing the overall performance of the catalyst. Further, the electrochemical sensor was prepared using CQDs@WS2/PANI nanocomposite on a paper-based electrode. The CQDs@WS2/PANI exhibit a linear response of 0.1–100 ​nM, with a limit of detection of 13 ​nM. This synergistic interfacial interaction resulted in the significantly improved electrochemical performance of the modified electrode. The proposed Z-scheme was justified by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and scavenger experiment. An intermediate degradation pathway was also proposed. The synthesized materials were characterized using FESEM, HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, UV-visible spectroscopy, PL, and TRPL. Therefore, this study provides a direct approach to fabricate a heterojunction that combines two-dimensional, one dimensional, and zero-dimensional properties, enabling control over the energy level configuration and subsequent improvements in photocatalytic and electrocatalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: The endocrine-disrupting Compound (EDCs) are a new class of hazardous pollutants causing negative effects on human beings and animals by disrupting the endocrine system. It has been reported that EDCs act as an endocrine disruptor and are associated with many reproductive and sexual disorders, low male sperm counts, mammalian carcinoma, and cardiovascular health [1,2]. Estradiol, an estrogen compound of natural/synthetic origin commonly used in the pharmaceutical industry for the treatment of diverse female reproductive disorders is recognized as an Endocrine-Disrupting Compound (EDC) [3]. The problem of water pollution and energy crisis has been considered an urgent global issue [4,5]. The persistence of pharmaceutical waste in water bodies increases the risk of endocrine disorders and antimicrobial resistance, poses a threat to global health [6]. A significant amount of estradiol eliminated in human and animal urine then contaminates water bodies and aquatic life. Consequently, estradiol has been considered as an emerging pollutant and a global concern [7]. On the Other hand, antibiotics are utilized in various fields such as agriculture, medicine, and animal husbandry, making them a widely exploited pharmacological category [8,9]. Antibiotic consumption has increased significantly over the past few decades as a result of the rising global population and pharmaceutical consumption [10,11]. In 2020, the global production of antibiotics reached 248,000 tons, nearly tripled since 2009. Medicines cannot be completely assimilated into the human body; therefore, the unabsorbed drugs are ultimately excreted as by-products. In addition, significant quantities of pharmaceutical waste are released from industry manufacturing, animal food, and improper discharge of expired pharmaceuticals [12,13]. Nitrofurantoin, a broadly employed antibiotic classified within the nitrofuran family, is typically used for treating the infections of urinary tract and respiratory. Nevertheless, it has been noted that excessive dosages of NFT may lead to adverse effects including dizziness, hepatotoxicity, depressive symptoms, and gastrointestinal distress. Consequently, certain nations have implemented stringent prohibitions on the utilization of NFT due to its carcinogenic and mutagenic potential [14,15].

Recently, the fabrication of composites with multiple components with high stability and efficiency has been considered an appealing strategy for enhancing the photocatalytic and electrocatalytic performance of semiconductors [16–18]. The efficiency of an individual catalyst with a high rate of carrier recombination, low surface area, and stability is lower than that of multiple catalysts [19]. However, the fabrication of binary and ternary composites with surface modifications, doping, and functionalization, provides effective strategies for enhancing catalytic activity [20,21]. The development of composite systems has attracted enormous attention due to their cost-effectiveness, higher quantum efficiency, low energy activation, and capacity to excite localised electronic states [22–24]. Recently, many reports have highlighted the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of ternary composites including MoO3/Fe2O3/r-GO [25], Ag/AgBr/BiOIO3 [26], CQDs/TiO2/ZnFe2O4 [21], NCQDs/WO3/GO [27] exhibited improved photocatalytic efficiency than the binary and pure materials.

Meanwhile, Z-scheme photocatalytic heterojunction systems offer a dual functionality: superior electron-hole separation efficiency and enhanced redox capability [28,29]. Previously, metals nanoparticles including copper (Cu), silver (Ag), gold (Au) and platinum (Pt) have been employed as electron mediators to enhance photocatalytic systems in Z-scheme heterojunctions [30]. Although these metal mediators exhibit high efficiency in electron transfer but accompanied by certain drawbacks including high cost and low stability [31,32]. Several Z-scheme heterojunction composites have been reported such as carbon quantum Dots/TiO2/g-C3N4 [33], PANI/Ag3PO4/NiFe2O4 [34], Carbon quantum Dots/Bi2MoO6/Graphitic Nanofibers [35], g-C3N4/WO3/AgI [36] and Bi2S3-Bi2WO6 [37]. Conversely, carbon quantum dots have the ability to play promising electron mediator with exceptional properties including electron reservoir, photo-induced charge transfer, up-converting, surface-functionalization, and high chemical stability [38,39]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit diverse surface functional groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl, and a conjugated carbon core. These functional moieties enable interactions with a range of molecules through coordinate covalent bonding, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic forces, and π-π interactions [40,41]. Therefore, the properties of CQDs can be modified by these interfacial interactions, allowing them to be used in several application.

Tungsten disulfide (WS2) is composed of S-M-S multilayer structure that are covalently bonded and layered via Van der Waals forces [42–44]. The narrow bandgap and layered structure of WS2 extended the efficiency of light absorption and facilitated the transfer of electrons in heterojunctions [45,46]. Further, conducting polymers (CPs) have gained tremendous attention in photodegradation and electrochemical sensing due to their excellent conductivity, high charge transfer efficiency with high sensitivity and selectivity. Polyaniline (PANI) has been extensively used among well-known conducting polymers due to its promising properties, including high conductivity, high solubility, and low cost [47–49]. Additionally, the inclusion of PANI to WS2 enhances the charge transfer property, surface area, and improve radical generation. Nevertheless, the materials were still experiencing rapid charge carrier recombination [50]. Therefore, an appropriate energy band structure is established through the synergistic coupling of WS2/PANI and CQDs. This configuration helps to enhance the performance of the catalyst.

In this study, we constructed a ternary nanocomposite (CQDs@WS2/PANI) by decorating CQDs to the WS2/PANI synthesized by green synthesis followed by in-situ polymerization. Therefore, this study provides a direct approach to fabricate a heterojunction that combines two-dimensional(2D), one dimensional(1D) and zero-dimensional(0D) properties, enabling control over the energy level configuration and subsequent improvements in photocatalytic and electrocatalytic efficiency. The improved photodegradation and electrocatalytic performance of the material and the underlying mechanism on the (Estradiol) EST and Nitrofurantoin (NFT) were analyzed.

Sodium tungstate dehydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), ammonium per sulfate ((NH4)2S2O8), and cetyl trialkyl ammonium bromide ([C16H33)N(CH3)3]Br) were supplied from Central Drug House, India. Thiourea (CH4N2S), Aniline (C6H5NH2), and hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OH·HCl) were acquired from Merck (P), India. The chemical substances employed in the experiments did not undergo any additional purification processes. All experimental procedures were conducted using DI water.

In an experiment, 60 ​mL of lemon juice extract was placed in an autoclave lined with Teflon and treated hydrothermally at 220 ​°C for 14 ​h as shown in Fig. 1 a. Following the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The obtained solution underwent a noticeable color transformation from off-white to dark brown, signifying the successful synthesis of CQDs. Further, larger nanoparticles were separated from these CQDs using 0.2 ​μm syringe filter.

The synthesis of WS2/PANI is a two-step process, hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the WS2 nanosheets which is subsequently followed by in-situ polymerization for the synthesis of WS2/PANI. Initially, ultrathin WS2 nanosheets were produced by dissolving 1.60 ​g of sodium tungstate and 1.45 ​g of thiourea in 30 ​mL of deionized (DI) water, which served as precursors. 0.60 ​g of hydroxylamine hydrochloride and 0.24 ​g of CTAB were added to this solution with continuously stirred for 1 ​h. Further, the solution was obtained and transferred to a 60 ​mL autoclave and subjected to a temperature of 180 ​°C for a duration of 24 ​h. WS2 nanosheets were obtained by subjecting the resulting dark black solution to multiple filtrations using DI water and ethanol, followed by drying at 60 ​°C for 12 ​h.

In the next step, a solution was obtained by combining 32 ​mL of DI water with 2 ​mL of aniline, and then adding 6 ​mL of HCl while stirring continuously. Further, the solution was freezed for 3 ​h. Following synthesis, the WS2 nanosheets were introduced into the solution after sonication for 30 ​min. As an oxidising agent, ammonium per sulfate was subsequently introduced drop by drop. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the solution was filtered, rinsed three times with DI water, and desiccated overnight at 50 ​°C following the completion of the polymerization process.

To make CQDs@WS2/PANI ternary composites, CQDs were added with the synthesized WS2/PANI nanocomposite, magnetically stirred for 12 ​h, and then ultrasonicated for 3 ​h, as shown in Fig. 1c. The mixtures finally dried at 60 ​°C in an oven. The synthesized sample was finally named as CQDs@WS2/PANI (WCP).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) study was carried out for structural investigation by employing Cu Kα radiation (λ ​= ​1.5406 ​Å) in a range of 10°–80° on a (Rigaku Smart Lab). Field emission scanning electron microscope (Quanta 3D FEG) and Transmission electron microscope (JEOL/JEM-F200) were performed for the morphological analysis. The optical analysis was performed on the UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Cary 100 series, Agilent Technologies) in the range of 200–1000 ​nm to determine the absorption spectrum of the synthesized material. The bonds, chemical states, and functional groups were analyzed by fourier transform infrared microscopy (Bruker's Vertex 70V) and XPS (ESCA+ ​Omicron Nano Technology). N2-adsorption-desorption isotherms and surface parameters were analyzed by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) at 77.4 ​K (NOVA-1000- version-370). The charge carrier recombination time study was performed by TCPCS (Horiba (DeltaFlex01-DD). The Electrochemical measurements (CV, EIS, mottschottky and DPV) was studied by Nova 2.0 Autolab (Metrohm). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra was done in the presence of DMPO as spin trapping agent acquired on a spectrometer (Jon Bruker BioSpin GmbH) for the detection of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals.

The two electrode system is comprised of conductive ink on paper based substrate with an electrode of reaction area of 1.8 ​mm2. After preparing the solution with CQDs@WS2/PANI (40 ​μL, 40 ​mg ​mL− 1 in ethanol), and sonicated to get a homogeneous suspension. Further, the 40 ​μL of the above suspension was delicately deposited to the cleaned surface of the paper electrode. The electrode was then dried under an infrared lamp to eliminate the solvent, leaving behind CQDs@WS2/PANI as the sensing platform.

In the initial step, a solution of EST and NFT with varying concentrations was prepared. Subsequently, 30 ​mg of the synthesized sample (WS2, WS2/PANI, and CQDs@WS2/PANI) was introduced into 100 ​mL of the solution (EST and NFT) and continuously stirred for 1 ​h in a dark environment to establish an equilibrium state of adsorption and desorption. Further, the solution underwent a photodegradation process, where it was exposed to a 350 ​W xenon lamp positioned 50 ​cm away from the beaker. The solution was maintained at continuous stirring during irradiation, and the incident light intensity reaching the solution was kept at approximately 100 ​mW ​cm2. After the photodegradation process, 2.5 ​mL of the solution underwent centrifugation at 1500 ​rpm for 10 ​min, and the resulting liquid supernatant was collected. UV-visible absorption spectra were then recorded at specified time intervals.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was carried out to confirm the hierarchical morphology of the nanocomposite. Fig. 2 a displayed the uniformly dispersed CQDs particles with the mean size of 3–4 ​nm (distribution in the insert). Fig. 2b showed the multiple nanosheets of WS2 with average length of ∼70 ​nm and interlayer spacing of 0.67 ​nm in the insert. Fig. 2c and d represents the ternary nanocomposite at a scale of 20 and 10 ​nm. The nanostructure appears to be highly transparent at a resolution of 10 ​nm. Fig. 2d showed coverage of WS2 nanosheets on the PANI nanofibers with CQDs on the surface of WS2. A close contact interface exists between WS2 and PANI, while the CQDs were dispersed uniformly on the nanocomposite. The HRTEM results provide compelling evidence for the successful formation of the ternary nanocomposite. The FESEM was further performed to investigate the surface morphology of all synthesized samples. As shown in Fig. 3 , pure WS2, PANI, and CQDs@WS2/PANI (WCP) nanohybrid morphology. Fig. 3a showed a FESEM image of WS2 nanostructure. It exhibited a clearly discernible sheet-like structure. A closer inspection revealed that the sheets-like nanostructure was comprised of clumped and interlocked nanosheets with ∼100 x 70 ​nm. The samples of pure PANI in Fig. 3b were fully composed of well-dispersed nanofiber with diameter of approximately 20 ​nm. For CQDs@WS2/PANI (WCP) nanocomposite, the nanofibers of PANI were interconnected on the surface of WS2(Fig. 3c). Compared with randomly dispersed pure PANI, the size of nanofibers of PANI in the composites became much smaller [51].

The surface chemical composition and bonding present on the surface of the CQDs@WS2/PANI were analyzed by XPS. The survey scan exhibited five peaks at 32.2 ​eV, 162.6 ​eV, 284.6 ​eV, 399.55 ​eV and 532.4 ​eV corresponding to W4f, S2p C1s, N1s, and O1s, which is in agreement with the chemical composition as represented in Fig. 4 a. The composition of the nanocomposite was comprised of W (17.7 %), S (2.23 ​%) carbon (75 ​%), Oxygen (5.9 ​%) and Nitrogen (5.2 ​%), respectively. Fig. 4b represented the high-resolution spectra of W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2 have been deconvoluted to reveal two distinct binding energy peaks at 32.72 and 34.90 ​eV, respectively. These peaks originate from the W(IV) oxidation state. A minor peak at 37.63 ​eV corresponding to W5p3/2 was also observed, signifying the presence of the W(VI) oxidation state [52]. Similarly, the S2p peaks associated with S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 ​at 163.2 ​eV and 164.3 ​eV, have been deconvoluted in Fig. 4c, in agreement with the previous work. Additionally, the peaks present at 161.4 ​eV and 162.3 ​eV were attributed to S2− state [53,54]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s for CQDs gives the formation of three chemical bonds, including C–C/C=C at 284.6 ​eV, -C=O at 285.9 ​eV, and -C-O at 288.4 ​eV, respectively (Fig. 4d). Further, O1s has three electronic states at 531.12 ​eV, 532.40 ​eV, and 533.72 ​eV corresponding to O-C=O, C=O and -C-O represented the oxygen moieties of CQDs (Fig. 4e). The structural constituents of PANI, are majorly comprised of nitrogen and carbon as its main components. High-resolution deconvolution spectra were performed for N1s to facilitate a comprehensive understanding of this analysis. Furthermore, in the N1s deconvoluted XPS spectrum, four distinct electronic states have been identified at binding energies of 398.26 ​eV, 399.50 ​eV, 400.69 ​eV, and 401.76 ​eV, attributed to quinoid amine (C-N), neutral amine (-NH-), benzenoid amine (C=N), and protonated amine (-NH+) (Fig. 4f) [55].

The surface elements of WS2, PANI, CQDs, and CQDs@WS2/PANI were analyzed by XPS survey scan in the range of 0–600 ​eV as shown in Fig. S1 a. To further understand the formation of CQDs@WS2/PANI(WCP) heterostructure, a comparison of W4f and S2p orbital of WS2 and WCP heterostructure were shown in Fig. S1 b and c. The analysis of the spectral features of W4f and S2p orbital was elucidated in Tables S1 and S2. The peak energy separation between a spin-orbit component of W4f is 2.19 ​eV. The binding energy of W4f7/2 (32.72 ​eV) and Wf5/2(34.90 ​eV) in WCP was shifted by 0.25 ​eV compared with that of pristine WS2, W4f7/2 (32.47 ​eV) and Wf5/2 (34.66 ​eV). In the case of S2p, the binding energy of S2p3/2 (163.2 ​eV) and S2p1/2 (164.3 ​eV) in WCP was shifted by 0.7 ​eV compared with that of pristine WS2, S2p3/2 (162.5 ​eV) and S2p1/2 (163.6 ​eV). Hence, this shifting in the orbital was ascribed to the electron transfer between each component of WCP. Additionally, the intensity in WCP was also observed to be reduced as compared to the pure WS2 that also signifying the interfacial interaction between WS2 and PANI as shown in Figs. S3b and c.

Fig. 5 a showed the XRD diffractogram for pure WS2, PANI, CQDs, and CQDs@WS2/PANI nanocomposite. The XRD of WS2 confirmed the formation of the hexagonal phase with lattice parameters a ​= ​b ​= ​0.34 ​nm and c ​= ​1.20 ​nm, consistent with the previously reported data (JCPDS file: 84–1398 and 87–2417). The diffraction peaks can be indexed as characteristic hexagonal phase with 14.70°, 28.95°, 30.48°, 35.35°, 38.61°, 46.09°, 50.40°, 53.21° and 60.35° corresponding to the planes (002), (004), (100), (102), (103), (104), (105), (106) and, (110), respectively. The observed sharp peaks of the synthesized nanosheets indicated the crystalline nature and calculated d spacing was 0.62 ​nm [56,57]. For PANI, the peaks at 15.06°, 20.02°, and 25.22° can be indexed as (011), (020), and (200) planes, respectively [48,58]. The XRD pattern of CQDs has a wide and weak diffraction peak positioned at 23.01°, with an estimated d-spacing of 0.39 ​nm. The abundance of functional groups present in the CQDs is responsible for the increased interlayer spacing [59]. Further, the XRD of CQDs@WS2/PANI showed no difference in the location of the diffraction peaks indicating the successful formation of CQDs@WS2/PANI ternary nanocomposite. Fig. 5b represented the functional moieties of the synthesized nanocomposite were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy. The observed peaks at 492–590 ​cm−1 and 877–1060 ​cm−1 were assigned to the presence of W-S and S-S bonds [60]. The peaks aroused due to OH stretching deformation and vibration were present at 1391 ​cm−1, 1702 ​cm−1, 2906 ​cm−1, and 2974 ​cm−1, respectively [56]. The peak at 1234 ​cm−1 present in nanocomposite was caused by the C-N stretching of aromatic amine, and the stretching caused by benzenoid and quinoid modes arises the peaks at 1398 ​cm−1 and 1560 ​cm−1, respectively. The C=N imine and the N=Q=N stretching modes give rise to the peaks at 1659 ​cm−1, and 2329 ​cm−1 respectively (Q: quinoid ring) [56,61]. The presence of the C=C bonds indicates that the CQDs have a graphitic structure, whereas the OH, C=O, and C-H peaks imply that the CQDs have surface moieties [27,62].

The UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy was essential to investigate the optical characteristics of the synthesized CQDs@WS2/PANI nanocomposite. The UV-Vis spectrum of WS2 exhibited two distinct characteristic peaks. The peak at 660 ​nm can be attributed to the characteristic excitonic transition owing to direct band gap energy, while the peak at 523 ​nm corresponds to the spin-split valence band-to-conduction band transition [63]. The band gap energy was also determined by employing Tauc's plot to examine the impact of incorporating PANI and CQDs into WS2. The calculated bandgap energy for the WS2 nanosheets was found to be 1.98 ​eV.as given in Fig. 6 a. The observed peak at 314 ​nm is assigned to the π–π∗ electronic transition generated due to the transition of electrons from HOMO to LUMO of the amine benzenoid rings, while another peak at 580 ​nm is attributed to the charge transfer between benzenoid–quinoid rings as illustrated in Fig. 6b [64,65]. The two absorption peaks were also obtained at 620 ​nm and 800 ​nm in WCP can be assigned to the presence of polaron and bipolaron band transitions of PANI [66,67]. In the case of CQDs, the peak around 300–400 ​nm is attributed to the n- π∗ transition of the C=O bond [28]. In the case of composite a slight peak shift was obtained and exhibited extended visible absorption in comparison to pure WS2. The estimated band gap of pure WS2, PANI, CQDs, and WCP are 1.98 ​eV, 2.81 ​eV, 3.18 ​eV, and 2.40 ​eV respectively. The decrease in bandgap energy could be attributed to the emergence of new excitation energy levels near the conventional bandgap, potentially resulting from charge transfer interactions between them [68].

The comparative photocatalytic performance of WS2, WS2/PANI, CQDs@WS2(CW), CQDs@PANI (CP) and, CQDs@WS2/PANI (WCP) was explored for the photodegradation of estradiol (EST) and nitrofurantoin (NFT). The experiment was done in the dark for 60 ​min to study the adsorption behaviour of the following photocatalyst. It was observed negligible degradation efficiency was obtained in dark using WS2, WS2/PANI, CW, CP and WCP which is 5.3 ​%, 10.8 ​%, 7.8 ​%, 8.4 ​% and 15.2 ​% for EST and 6 ​%, 12.1 ​%, 8.1 ​%, 10.6 ​% and 12.6 ​% for NFT in 60 ​min, respectively. Fig. 7 a and d represented the C/C0 vs time plots. Fig. 7 c and f. illustrated that WCP showed the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 96.63 ​% for EST in 60 ​min and 95.76 ​% for NFT in 60 ​min. However, WS2, WS2/PANI, CW and CP exhibited substantially lower degradation efficiencies, reaching only 18.64 %, 58.06 %, 52.20 ​% and 56.31 ​% for EST and, 28.19 %, 70.54 ​%, 84.10 ​% and 87.8 ​% for NFT, respectively, under identical experimental conditions. The electrons present in the CB of WS2 cannot be scavenged because of the lower oxidation potential and causes rapid recombination of charge carriers, thus lowers photocatalytic activity [27]. The integration of PANI enhances degradation due to the scavenging of charge carriers that reduces the rate of recombination and the position of the CB edge to provide enough potential for the production of reactive oxygen species [69,70]. In addition, the inclusion of CQDs extended the light absorption in visible spectrum and helps in electron trapping and hindered the electron-hole recombination [71]. This electron scavenging property of CQDs and PANI combined with charge transfer capability of CQDs to improve the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite.

The rate kinetics of the photocatalysis were also investigated using the first-order kinetics equation [1]:
[1]
ln
C
C 0
= - kt

Here, k represented the first order rate constant, C and C0 are the concentration at time 't' min, and '0' min, respectively. Fig. 7b and e indicated that -ln(C/C0) changes linearly with irradiation time, confirming that the photocatalytic reaction adhered to first-order rate kinetics. The obtained rate constants for WS2, WS2/PANI, and CQDs@WS2/PANI were 0.0025 min−1, 0.0101 min−1, 0.0184 min−1 for EST and 0.0048 min−1, 0.0183 min−1 and 0.0476 min−1 for NFT, respectively. The WCP exhibited the highest degradation rate constant as compared to other photocatalysts and hence the photocatalytic efficiency. As shown in Table 1 , a correlation was found to be observed between rate constant and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency, larger the rate constant highest will be the degradation efficiency. As illustrated in Fig. S2 a and b and Table S3, the rate constant(k) was normalized with the BET surface area. The highest value of normalized rate constant(k) per surface area was for WCP in both the cases.

The photocatalytic activity was also investigated by taking different concentrations of NFT and EST using CQDs@WS2/PANI as shown in Fig. 8 . The obtained degradation efficiencies at various concentrations in 60 ​min is 1 ​mg (99.8 ​%), 5 ​mg (99.6 ​%), 10 ​mg (98.3 ​%), and 15 ​mg (96.6 ​%) for NFT. Similarly, degradation efficiency for estradiol is 0.2 ​mg (96.7 ​%) 0.4 ​mg (68.8 ​%), 0.6 ​mg (45.7 ​%) and 0.8 ​mg (32.3 ​%) in 60 ​min, respectively. The rate kinetics study using pseudo first order model, pseudo second order model and intra-diffusion kinetics models was also studies for various concentration of EST and NFT (Fig. S3 and Table S4, (ESI)).

Liquid chromatography mass spectroscopy (LC-MS) analysis was studied to examine the intermediate degradation products and pathways of estradiol and nitrofurantoin as shown in Figs. 9 and 10
. The Photolytic degradation of nitrofurantoin (NFT) (238) resulted in two primary proposed degradation products: 4-nitrocyclopenta-1,3-dienecarbaldehyde (m/z:141) and 1-aminoimidazolidine-2,4-dione (m/z:115), respectively. The chromatogram of NFT and EST degradation after 20 ​min is provided in the (Fig. S4 ESI), showing various peaks corresponding to intermediates. To investigate degradation pathways, degraded product structures were proposed based on mass spectra (m/z) values. The reaction was initiated by photohydrolysis, which transformed the parent molecular ion (NFT) into two potential intermediates. 5-(hydroxymethylene)furan-2-one (m/z:112) was produced by the subsequent degradation of m/z:141, whereas m/z:115 exhibited no reactivity and undergo total degradation [72]. Using condensation or substitution reaction, m/z:112 was converted to C1(5)-oxybisfuran-2-carbaldehyde (m/z:206). Further pathways involved photochemical hydroxylation, yielding a transformation product with a molecular ion at m/z:222. It is probable that reactions involving reversible substitution and reduction took place at reactive positions within the two furan rings, resulting in the generation of photodegradation product signals at m/z 178 and 150, respectively (Fig. 9). The proposed mechanism outlines three primary processes for the formation of NFT products: the opening of a heterocyclic ring followed by hydrolysis; the nucleophilic substitution of aromatic bonds; and the homolytic cleavage of N–N bonds. Following the homolytic cleavage of the carbon–carbon single bond in the five-membered heterocyclic ring, the intermediate amide is decarbonylated and hydrolyzed [14,73].

The photocatalytic degradation of estradiol (EST) yielded two open-chain metabolites, which possessed mass-to-charge ratios (m/z) of 336 and 354, respectively (Fig. 10). According to the literature, the estrogenic activity of EST is ascribed to its phenolic group, it is critical to disrupt this group in order to reduce the estrogenic impacts on river water. The proposed mechanism suggests the formation of aliphatic and hydroxylated byproducts, with 2-Hydroxyestradiol (m/z: 288) identified as a significant degradation product exhibiting nearly an 18-fold reduction in estrogenic activity compared to EST. The dominant pathway implies hydroxyl radicals attacking EST, which subsequently contributes a hydroxyl radical to the EST ring. The primary site for the initial hydroxyl radical addition was identified at the 2nd carbon atom. This process involves ring opening, yielding oxidized products with dicarboxylic acids (m/z: 336 and 352). In an alternative pathway, the byproduct with m/z: 336 undergoes hydroxyl radical attack, leading to the formation of m/z 354 through hydroxyl addition [74–76]. Subsequently, several multistep oxidation and reduction processes occur, facilitating the degradation of the pollutant molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge transfer and surface area analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence spectroscopy helps to investigate the transfer of electrons (e−) and holes (h+) and the recombination occurs in charge carriers excited by the irradiation of the nanocomposite. Fig. 11 a demonstrated the photoluminescence spectra of WS2, WS2/PANI and CQDs@WS2/PANI. The peak observed around 429 ​nm is indicative of the π∗–π transition of the benzenoid units and de-excitation of the polaron band, respectively [67]. In case of WS2 nanosheets, the sharp peak with a shoulder at 456 ​nm can be assigned to the quantum-confined transitions [63]. It is evident from the PL spectra that the addition of PANI and CQDs causes substantial PL quenching as compared to WS2, indicating a significant reduction in electron–hole recombination. This supports the increased quantum effects of the synthesized composite and demonstrates that the e− are effectively transferred from the surface of WS2 to PANI and CQDs [71]. Consequently, the recombination of charge carriers is drastically reduced, resulting in an increase in photocatalytic activity.

Similarly, the decrease in recombination rate was also justified by Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) analysis as shown in Fig. 11b. The recombination time of the synthesized material was determined by TCSPC.

Upon irradiating the WCP material, electrons transition from the valence band to the conduction band, creating electron-hole (e-h+) pairs. This process results in the spatial separation of the photoinduced electrons (e−) and holes (h+).

The charge transfer and the time duration of photon emission (called the decay time) were analyzed using tri-exponential fitting (Eq. (2))

The intensity of photoluminescence (PL) is denoted by I(t), with A1, A2, and A3 represented as amplitude constants. τ1 corresponds to the slower component associated with radiative recombination, while τ2 and τ3 represented the faster components attributed to non-radiative recombination [77]. The average lifetime (τ avg) was subsequently determined by the equation given below [Eq. (3)]:
[3]

The observed PL may be originated from the defects or interfacial charge transfer states near the conduction band (CB) and can form PL emission centres. The observed TRPL kinetics are clearly consistent with the PL data, indicating that these states receive and accumulate the photogenerated electrons transported from the CB bottom in a tri-exponential relaxation manner. Here, it is important to emphasize that in the presence of PANI and CQDs, the average life time characterizing the overall electron transfer from CB to the stable state is lengthened from 2.87 ns (WS2) to 8.91 ns (CQDs@WS2/PANI), respectively. It is acceptable to infer that the increased lifetime of charge carriers in the photocatalysis may provide more opportunities for photogenerated electrons to participate in the photocatalytic degradation mechanism [78].

The interfacial photoinduced charge transfer characteristics of the composite material were also examined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements (Fig. 11c). Significantly, the composite material incorporating CQDs (WCP) exhibited the smallest radius (WCP:1.749 ​kΩ < WS2/PANI:3.471 ​kΩ < WS2:21.848 ​kΩ), indicating an increased rate of charge carrier transfer and a decreased charge transfer resistance [79]. The results obtained from these experiments were consistent with the analysis of photoluminescence (PL) spectra, indicating that CQDs enable remarkable photogenerated charge separation. The electron transfer at the composite interface is further accelerated by this improvement in charge separation, which is consistent with the results of the aforementioned photocatalytic performance.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) technique were analyzed to determine the specific surface area and pore structure of the synthesized material. As illustrated in Fig. 11d, WS2, WS2/PANI and CQDs@WS2/PANI displayed the type-IV isotherms with H-4 hysteresis loops which are characteristics of mesoporous materials. The obtained surface areas were 7.54 ​m2/g for WS2, 25.02 for WS2/PANI and 28.21 ​m2/g for CQDs@WS2/PANI, respectively. The difference implies that the specific surface area of the catalysts has been significantly increased due to the incorporation of PANI and CQDs. The catalysts with larger surface area have the potential for more adsorption and active sites, leading to better photocatalytic activity.

The elucidation of the photocatalytic degradation mechanism was carried out by performing scavenger experiments to capture reactive oxygen species (ROS) as shown in Fig. 12 a. The scavengers used for radical capture were potassium iodide (KI), benzoquinone (BZQ), and tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH), responsible for hydroxyl (•OH) radicals, and holes, •O2 − radicals and hydroxyl (•OH) radicals, respectively. The photodegradation efficiency of WCP against NFT was calculated to be ∼80 ​% within first 30-min, in the absence of scavengers. Nevertheless, the photodegradation rate experienced a reduction of 64.1 ​%, 54.7 ​%, and 41.2 ​% in the presence of t-BuOH, KI, and BZQ, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 12a. The obtained result provides compelling evidence that •O2 − radicals are the dominating radical responsible for inducing photodegradation. In order to evaluate the recyclability of the photocatalyst, the photodegradation experiment was conducted in a systematic manner, ensuring that consistent conditions were maintained throughout each cycle. After completing each cycle, the photocatalyst was subjected to washing, drying, and subsequent application. As shown in Fig. 12b, the photocatalyst retained its degradation efficiency, exhibiting no appreciable decrease even after four cycles. The stability of the photocatalyst was also explored by analyzing the XRD of the nanocomposite before and after the photocatalysis (Fig. S6). Thus, it has been demonstrated that the synthesized CQDs@WS2/PANI were effective and stable photocatalyst. Further, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was employed to confirm the presence of generated free radicals during the photocatalytic degradation process. It is evident from Fig. 12c and d that in the absence of illumination, negligible signal of free radicals was present. However, after exposure to light, distinct peaks corresponding to •OH and •O2ˉ was obtained [23]. The observed intensity •O2ˉ radical was high as compared to •OH radical which is in agreement of the scavenger experiment.

To further analyze the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the Z-scheme CQDs@WS2/PANI ternary nanocomposite, an analysis of the heterojunction structure was conducted. The semiconductor characteristics of WS2 and PANI, including their flat band, were determined through Mott-Schottky analysis as shown in Fig. 13 . The semiconductor is categorized as n-type or p-type based on the slope of the Mott-Schottky curve; a positive slope indicates n-type classification, while a negative slope indicates p-type classification [34]. It was found that both WS2 and PANI displayed properties that are typical of n-type semiconductors. Furthermore, based on the Mott-Schottky curves, the flat band level of WS2 was determined to be ​∼ ​−0.04 ​eV, while that of PANI was found to be −1.75 ​eV. The subsequent step involved the use of the flat band potential of WS2 and PANI to calculate the conduction band and valence band potentials, employing the equation [4&5]:
[4]

V fb

V CB

E
g
The determined valence band and conduction band positions for WS2 were 2.01 ​eV and −0.09 ​eV, respectively, while for PANI, they 1.44 ​eV and −1.66 ​eV, respectively.

In the initial stages, WS2 and PANI capture the visible light energy to generate the electron-hole pairs. The electrons originating from the valence bands migrate to the conduction bands in both semiconductors (WS2 and PANI). Subsequently, the majority of these electrons residing in the conduction band of WS2 primarily migrate to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (WS2 → CQDs), which are uniformly dispersed on the WS2/PANI nanocomposite, thereby minimizing charge transfer distances. Subsequently, holes located in the valence band of PANI combine with these electrons via a Z-scheme pathway (WS2→CQDs→PANI) [13,81]. Thus, the confirmation of Z-scheme was also confirmed from EPR and scavenger experiment which showed the similar results. The electrons associated with the conduction band of PANI generates the superoxide radicals (•O2 −) by reacting with oxygen, which subsequently directly attack pollutant molecules or further transform into hydroxyl radicals (•OH) for degradation. Simultaneously, the holes residing in the valence band of WS2 react with hydroxide ions to produce hydroxyl radicals or oxidize organic substances [82]. This mechanism confirms the formation of Z-scheme.

This photodegradation process can be described as follows [6–15]:

Additionally, TRPL experiment emphasize that in the presence of PANI and CQDs, the average life time characterizing the overall electron transfer from CB to the stable state is lengthened from 2.87 ns (WS2) to 8.91 ns (CQDs@WS2/PANI), respectively. It is acceptable to infer that the increased lifetime of charge carriers is due to the scavenging of electron at the interface due to the formation of Z-Scheme.

The Z-scheme configuration of the CQDs@WS2/PANI heterostructure system not only facilitates the separation of photoinduced charge carriers but also enhances light absorption and energy utilization. The implementation of multistep electron transfer processes significantly reduces recombination, thereby enhancing the efficiency of charge separation. Consequently, the photocatalytic degradation of EST and NFT is potentially enhanced by the synergistic effect of the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism.

The investigation of the electron transfer process and the electrochemical sensing mechanism can be studied by varying the scan rate. In Fig. 15 a scan rate was studied by depositing CQDs@WS2/PANI(WCP) on the electrode. The increase in scan rate is directly proportional to current as given in Fig. 15b. Further, square root of scan rate and log of scan rate is increased linearly with current with R2 ​= ​0.99 and 0.98, showed the process is diffusion-controlled Fig. 15c and d. After that, scan rate was investigated with estradiol on the WCP modified electrode. As the scan rate increased, the oxidation peak potential moved to higher potential showing that the electrochemical oxidation occurs on the surface of the electrode was controlled by ion adsorption and diffusion (Fig. 16 ). Further, the effects of scan rate were also investigated to assure the interaction between WCP and estradiol was governed by adsorption or diffusion-controlled mechanism. The scan rates with the corresponding current signals were plotted in accordance with Eq. (16) based on the surface adsorption process theory as shown in Fig. 16b [83].
(16)

where Ip is the peak current (A), n denotes the number of electrons, F is the Faraday's constant (96,485C ​mol− 1), V is the scan rate (V⋅s−1), A is the effective surface area of the electrode (cm2), GЙ represented the adsorbed species (mol∙cm− 2), R is the universal gas constant (R ​= ​8.314 ​J ​K− 1 ​mol− 1), and T is the temperature. Further, to justify the diffusion process mechanism, a plot of current against the square root of scan rates was employed, following the Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (17) [84])
(17)

where C0 represented the concentration of the analyte (mol⋅cm− 3) and D0 is the diffusion coefficient (cm2 ⋅s− 1). The obtained electrochemical oxidation peak current increased linearly as the scan rate increases with the linear equation: Ip ​= ​0.086V–0.4058, R2 ​= ​0.99 as shown in Fig. 16b. The linear equation for current vs the square root of scan rates is: Ip ​= ​1.1583V1/2–3.8504, R2 ​= ​0.99. (Fig. 16c). The obtained correlation coefficient (R2) was almost similar in both the cases, which implied that the electrochemical oxidation reaction for the sensing of estradiol by WCP was equally supported by surface adsorption and diffusion-controlled processes.

The logarithm of current vs scan rate was plotted to further confirm which process was more dominating in controlling the estradiol sensing. The slopes in adsorption-and diffusion-controlled processes should ideally have values of 1.0 and 0.5, respectively [85]. The obtained linear equation was: log Ip ​= ​1.126logV – 1.339, R2 ​= ​0.99 (Fig. 16 d) which showed the slope of 1.126 is closer to 1.0 than 0.5. Consequently, the adsorption process predominantly controls the electrochemical sensing of estradiol using WCP. This dominance of adsorption can be attributed to the various interactions such as π-π stacking, electrostatic interactions, and hydrogen bonding (Fig. 17 ).

Further, the Laviron's equation [84] (Eq. (18) was used to determine the number of electron transfer occurs in the electrochemical reaction mechanism between estradiol and WCP(Eq. (18)

The regression equation was obtained from the plot of oxidation peak potential (Epa) vs lnV was Epa ​= ​0.0437lnV ​+0.37736 (R2 ​= ​0.99) (Fig. S5 ESI). The slope of αn =0.43 was obtained from the equation. Thus, the oxidation of estradiol by WCP involves the transfer of one-electron, which is consistent with previous research.

The sensing of estradiol was done using CQDs@WS2/PANI (WCP) by differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV). The DPV curve of WCP showed very small oxidation peak but in the presence of estradiol, the oxidation current increased at 0.82 ​V suggesting that estradiol undergo oxidiation by the WCP modified electrode as shown in Fig. 18 a. A carbonyl group was formed by oxidising the hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring of the estradiol molecule, which caused an electron transfer to the electrode leading to an increased current response as shown in Fig. 17. The signals of current generated due to the oxidation of estradiol, significantly diminished in the absence of estradiol in the WCP-modified electrode, as confirmed by the control experiment. The sensing performance of WCP was also investigated by using various concentrations of estradiol (100–0.1 ​nM) in PBS at pH 7 Fig. 18b. The electrochemical oxidation peak current was found to be increased as we increase the concentration of estradiol (Fig. 18c). The linear plot of current vs potential (100–0.1 ​nM) was used to determine the limit of detection(LOD). The obtained LOD was 13 ​nM using 3σ/slope, where σ is standard deviation.

The selectivity of CQDs@WS2/PANI modified electrode was investigated using several interferants such as alprazolam, paracetamol, ascorbic acid and Urea as shown in Fig. 18d. The following interferants were mixed with the estradiol and the DPV signal was measured. The mixture solution was compared with the pure estradiol solution with same concentration and pH and it was observed that the change in the current signals were negligible. The relative change in the current signals in the presence and absence of interferants in estradiol solution were negligible which shows the good selectivity of the fabricated electrodes.

In conclusion, the efficient Z-scheme ternary nano-heterojunctions CQDs@WS2/PANI was fabricated by green synthesis followed by in-situ polymerization. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent characteristics including efficient charge separation, broad-spectrum light absorption, and high surface area which contributed the high photocatalytic performance, exceptional stability, and selectivity. Further, the synergistic combination of the ternary composite yielded a sensing platform with high selectivity, and rapid detection capabilities for estradiol. This process was facilitated by favorable energy levels and controlled mainly by adsorption mechanisms involving π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding between 17β-estradiol and the CQDs@WS2/PANI interfaces. The nanocomposite showed the high sensitivity over a wide linear range from 100 to 0.1 ​nm, with an exceptionally low detection limit of 13 ​nm. Thus, the present study help in the development of novel strategies for designing advanced Z-scheme photocatalysts and electrocatalysts by modifying and controlling interface engineering to achieve high efficiency.
